  ### Kyle Brazeau
  
  1w
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/george-netscher-a4696b59_director-of-ai-computer-vision-safelyyou-activity-7449206953500762112-76TI)
  
  I'm getting to that age where I'm hearing stories from friends about their parents' struggles with Alzheimer's. What if your AI models could actually help someone’s parents or grandparents stay safe? 👀 Here at SafelyYou, we are a fast-growing, well-funded startup hiring a Director of AI (Computer Vision) - fully remote. This isn’t a “train a model, ship it, move on” kind of role. This is:
  → Setting the AI direction for the entire company 💥 → Building and scaling real products (not just experiments)
  → Using computer vision to genuinely improve the lives of seniors across North America
  🔊 Translation: your work will matter to real people and make a tangible difference in lives. We have strong ARR growth, investors chomping at the bit, and the kind of momentum that makes things…fun 🚀 Looking for someone who can zoom out strategically and zoom in technically without breaking a sweat.
  🔥 If you'd crush this or know someone who would, reach out or take a look 👇 [https://lnkd.in/gz9Kycbc](https://lnkd.in/gz9Kycbc?trk=public_profile__posts-text) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/george-netscher-a4696b59_director-of-ai-computer-vision-safelyyou-activity-7449206953500762112-76TI) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/george-netscher-a4696b59_director-of-ai-computer-vision-safelyyou-activity-7449206953500762112-76TI)

  ### UC Berkeley Sutardja Center for Entrepreneurship and Technology (SCET)
  
  1mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sutardjacenter_ai-leadership-ucberkeley-activity-7432847066512539648-0CgF)
  
  The future of AI isn’t just about the tech—it’s about the leadership behind it. 🚀
  We are thrilled to announce the AI Leadership Accelerator at [UC Berkeley College of Engineering](https://www.linkedin.com/school/berkeleyengineering/?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , happening April 13–15, 2026. This 3-day executive-intensive is designed for leaders who are ready to move beyond the hype and into high-impact AI strategy.
  Led by world-renowned faculty directors [Pieter Abbeel](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pieterabbeel?trk=public_profile__posts-text) (Visionary & Global AI Leader) and [David Gallacher](https://www.linkedin.com/in/davidcgallacher?trk=public_profile__posts-text) (Business Strategy Expert), you will gain the frameworks necessary to:
  ➡️ Build a robust AI roadmap for your enterprise.
  ➡️ Navigate complex governance and ethical frameworks.
  ➡️ Drive innovation across every business function.
  Join us in person at [University of California, Berkeley](https://www.linkedin.com/school/uc-berkeley/?trk=public_profile__posts-text) to learn from the minds shaping the future of the industry. The countdown is on - [https://hubs.ly/Q043Y5Z80](https://hubs.ly/Q043Y5Z80?trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fai&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fleadership&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fucberkeley&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fexecutiveeducation&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdigitaltransformation&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [\](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Finnovation&trk=public_profile__posts-text) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sutardjacenter_ai-leadership-ucberkeley-activity-7432847066512539648-0CgF) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sutardjacenter_ai-leadership-ucberkeley-activity-7432847066512539648-0CgF)

  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_can-humanoids-perform-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%97%B4%F0%9D%97%B6%F0%9D%97%B9%F0%9D%97%B2-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%81%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BB%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BA%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%80-activity-7429555267656130560-URHq)
  
  [Lujie Yang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/lujie-yang)
  
  ### Lujie Yang
  
  2mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_can-humanoids-perform-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%97%B4%F0%9D%97%B6%F0%9D%97%B9%F0%9D%97%B2-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%81%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BB%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BA%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%80-activity-7429555267656130560-URHq)
  
  Can humanoids perform 𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗹𝗲, 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗼𝘂𝘀, 𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠-𝗵𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘇𝗼𝗻 parkour based on what they see in the world?
  We present 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗼𝘂𝗿 (𝗣𝗛𝗣): a modular framework that chains dynamic human skills using onboard depth perception.
  Goal: perception in → parkour actions out.
  Method: long-horizon trajectory synthesis via motion matching → RL motion tracking → large-scale depth-conditioned, multi-skill distillation.
  Try the policy yourself! [https://lnkd.in/geiV95vJ](https://lnkd.in/geiV95vJ?trk=public_profile__posts-text) Project website: [https://lnkd.in/gVx-Q8Wb](https://lnkd.in/gVx-Q8Wb?trk=public_profile__posts-text) Huge shout out to the amazing team: [Zhen Wu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/zhen-wu-326a70230?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Xiaoyu Huang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/xiaoyu-huang-03a15822a?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Yuanhang Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yuanhangz?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , Koushil Sreenath, [Peter Chen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/peter-xi-chen?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Pieter Abbeel](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pieterabbeel?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Rocky Duan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rocky-duan-1a82662a?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Angjoo Kanazawa](https://www.linkedin.com/in/angjoo-kanazawa-54900918?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Carmelo (Carlo) Sferrazza](https://www.linkedin.com/in/csferrazza?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Guanya Shi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/guanya?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Karen Liu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karen-liu-a132875?trk=public_profile__posts-text) from Amazon FAR (Frontier AI & Robotics), UC Berkeley, Stanford, and CMU! This work was done during an internship at Amazon FAR. [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_can-humanoids-perform-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%97%B4%F0%9D%97%B6%F0%9D%97%B9%F0%9D%97%B2-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%81%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BB%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BA%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%80-activity-7429555267656130560-URHq) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_can-humanoids-perform-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%97%B4%F0%9D%97%B6%F0%9D%97%B9%F0%9D%97%B2-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%81%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BB%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BA%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%80-activity-7429555267656130560-URHq)
  
  [public\_profile\_\_posts](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_can-humanoids-perform-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%97%B4%F0%9D%97%B6%F0%9D%97%B9%F0%9D%97%B2-%F0%9D%97%AE%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%81%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BB%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%97%BA%F0%9D%97%BC%F0%9D%98%82%F0%9D%98%80-activity-7429555267656130560-URHq)

* [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pieterabbeel?trk=public_profile__posts_face-pile-cta)
  
    + Report this post
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/csferrazza_sim-to-real-learning-for-humanoid-robots-activity-7401394244411711488-DruB)
  
  [Carmelo (Carlo) Sferrazza](https://www.linkedin.com/in/csferrazza)
  
  ### Carmelo (Carlo) Sferrazza
  
  4mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/csferrazza_sim-to-real-learning-for-humanoid-robots-activity-7401394244411711488-DruB)
  
  Sim-to-real learning for humanoid robots is a full-stack problem. Today, Amazon FAR is releasing a full-stack solution: Holosoma. To accelerate research, we are open-sourcing a complete codebase covering multiple simulation backends, training, retargeting, and real-world inference. [https://lnkd.in/dUC93Y\_q](https://lnkd.in/dUC93Y_q?trk=public_profile__posts-text) Simulation frameworks such as IsaacGym, IsaacLab, MuJoCo Playground, mjlab were all great inspiration to us! With Holosoma, we unify the simulation landscape: IsaacGym, IsaacSim, and MJWarp backends are all supported in a single training codebase. We also support multiple robots and RL algorithms: PPO and FastSAC for very efficient learning — and offer native multi-GPU support!
  A major barrier in humanoid research is the lack of fully open, low-latency inference pipelines. Therefore, we release our inference stack, which allows you to run the exact same code across simulation backends and on real-world robots. We support both velocity tracking and whole-body tracking tasks. For whole-body tracking, we address retargeting with an OmniRetarget ( [https://lnkd.in/dP2Rm-js](https://lnkd.in/dP2Rm-js?trk=public_profile__posts-text) ) open-source implementation and re-implement simple BeyondMimic-style ( [https://lnkd.in/dgQbPj\_m](https://lnkd.in/dgQbPj_m?trk=public_profile__posts-text) ) tracking.
  A feature I find really helpful is video logging to wandb across all simulation backends. So much easier to sweep hyperparameters and browse through the results when you get to actually see the policy behavior on the screen. We also log ONNX files as we train, and our inference pipeline supports loading them directly through wandb, which makes sim-to-real iterations astonishingly fast!
  We focused heavily on simplicity and modularity. Reward engineering is kept to a minimum, and we support manager-based environments (similar to Isaac Lab and mjlab) for ease of adaptability. In fact, Holosoma was built to be extended! We are releasing an example extension showing how to adapt the relevant classes to support training and inference on a Unitree Go2 quadruped robot: [https://lnkd.in/du5a3-C8](https://lnkd.in/du5a3-C8?trk=public_profile__posts-text) Our goal is to lower the barrier to entry for humanoid research. By providing a broad infrastructure backbone, we hope to empower researchers to focus on novel algorithms and behaviors.
  This was all built by amazing people at Amazon FAR: [Pieter Abbeel](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pieterabbeel?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Juyue Chen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/juyuechen?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Rocky Duan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rocky-duan-1a82662a?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Alejandro Escontrela](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alejandro-escontrela?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Manan Gandhi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/manan-a-gandhi?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Samuel Gundry](https://au.linkedin.com/in/samuelgundry?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , Xiaoyu Huang, [Angjoo Kanazawa](https://www.linkedin.com/in/angjoo-kanazawa-54900918?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Tomasz Lewicki](https://www.linkedin.com/in/t-lewicki?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Jiaman Li](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jiaman-li-161b73164?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Karen Liu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karen-liu-a132875?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Clay Rosenthal](https://www.linkedin.com/in/clay-rosenthal?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Younggyo Seo](https://www.linkedin.com/in/younggyo-seo-52671413a?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Guanya Shi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/guanya?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Linda Shih](https://www.linkedin.com/in/linda--shih?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Jonathan Tseng](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jonathan-y-tseng?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Zhen Wu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/zhen-wu-326a70230?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Lujie Yang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/lujie-yang?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Brent Yi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brentyi?trk=public_profile__posts-text) , [Yuanhang Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yuanhangz?trk=public_profile__posts-text) All of this is open-source, and you can get started now at: [https://lnkd.in/dUC93Y\_q](https://lnkd.in/dUC93Y_q?trk=public_profile__posts-text) Example extension for Go2 robot: [https://lnkd.in/du5a3-C8](https://lnkd.in/du5a3-C8?trk=public_profile__posts-text) We can't wait to see what the robotics community builds with Holosoma! [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/csferrazza_sim-to-real-learning-for-humanoid-robots-activity-7401394244411711488-DruB) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/csferrazza_sim-to-real-learning-for-humanoid-robots-activity-7401394244411711488-DruB)

* [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pieterabbeel?trk=public_profile__posts_face-pile-cta)
  
    + Report this post
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_ive-long-wondered-if-we-can-make-a-humanoid-activity-7382429728026075136-vJdC)
  
  [Lujie Yang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/lujie-yang)
  
  ### Lujie Yang
  
  6mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_ive-long-wondered-if-we-can-make-a-humanoid-activity-7382429728026075136-vJdC)
  
  I've long wondered if we can make a humanoid robot do a 𝘄𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗽 - and we just made it happen by leveraging 𝗢𝗺𝗻𝗶𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗲𝘁 with BeyondMimic tracking!
  This came after our original OmniRetarget experiments, with only minor tweaks to RL training: relaxing a termination threshold and removing one reward term.
  The policy achieved a 𝟱/𝟱 success rate in our real-world experiments, showing the strength of high-quality, interaction-preserving motion retargeting combined with minimal RL tracking.
  Here is the updated arXiv: [https://lnkd.in/eNtWz\_fz](https://lnkd.in/eNtWz_fz?trk=public_profile__posts-text) (In Sec. V. A) and website: [https://lnkd.in/e\_8eMMtp](https://lnkd.in/e_8eMMtp?trk=public_profile__posts-text) .
  Original post: [https://lnkd.in/eNMZfQ88](https://lnkd.in/eNMZfQ88?trk=public_profile__posts-text) . [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_ive-long-wondered-if-we-can-make-a-humanoid-activity-7382429728026075136-vJdC) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lujie-yang_ive-long-wondered-if-we-can-make-a-humanoid-activity-7382429728026075136-vJdC)

  [Peter Chen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/peter-xi-chen)
  
  ### Peter Chen
  
  6mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/peter-xi-chen_very-excited-to-welcome-yuri-makarov-nikita-activity-7379926860693999616-aMaY)
  
  Very excited to welcome [Yuri Makarov](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/lvoursl?trk=public_profile__posts-text) [Nikita Karaev](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/nikitakaraev?trk=public_profile__posts-text) [Eldar Insafutdinov](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/eldar-insafutdinov-18845315?trk=public_profile__posts-text) [Laurynas Karazija](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/laurynas-karazija-b9591b103?trk=public_profile__posts-text) to Amazon FAR! [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/peter-xi-chen_very-excited-to-welcome-yuri-makarov-nikita-activity-7379926860693999616-aMaY)
  
  [Yuri Makarov](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/lvoursl)
  
  ### Yuri Makarov
  
  6mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/peter-xi-chen_very-excited-to-welcome-yuri-makarov-nikita-activity-7379926860693999616-aMaY)
  
  Excited to share that our team is joining the Frontier AI and Robotics (FAR) lab at [Amazon](https://www.linkedin.com/company/amazon?trk=public_profile__posts-text) to continue building the future of robotics. This feels like the start of a very special chapter for us, and we’ll be sharing more soon about how our research evolves.   [Reflection](https://www.linkedin.com/company/reflectionai)
  
  ### Reflection
  
  6mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel reposted this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/reflectionai_today-were-sharing-the-next-phase-of-reflection-activity-7382075993852870656-9Bcn)
  
  Today we're sharing the next phase of Reflection.
  We're building frontier open intelligence accessible to all.
  We've assembled an extraordinary AI team, built a frontier LLM training stack, and raised $2 billion.
  Technological and scientific progress is driven by values of openness and collaboration.
  The internet, Linux, and the protocols and standards that underpin modern computing are all open. This isn't a coincidence. Open software is what gets forked, customized, and embedded into systems worldwide. It's what universities teach, what startups build on, what enterprises deploy.
  Open science enables others to learn from the results, be inspired by them, interrogate them, and build upon them in order to push the frontier of human knowledge and scientific advancement. AI got to where it is today through scaling ideas (e.g. self-attention, next token prediction, reinforcement learning) that were shared and published openly.
  Now AI is becoming the technology layer that everything else runs on top of. The systems that accelerate scientific research, enhance education, optimize energy usage, supercharge medical diagnoses, and run supply chains will all be built on AI infrastructure.
  But the frontier is currently concentrated in closed labs. If this continues, a handful of entities will control the capital, compute, and talent required to build AI, creating a runaway dynamic that locks everyone else out. There's a narrow window to change this trajectory. We need to build open models so capable that they become the obvious choice for users and developers worldwide, ensuring the foundation of intelligence remains open and accessible rather than controlled by a few.
  Over the last year, we've been preparing for this mission.
  We’ve assembled a team who have pioneered breakthroughs including PaLM, Gemini, AlphaGo, AlphaCode, AlphaProof, and contributed to ChatGPT and Character AI, among many others.
  We built something once thought possible only inside the world’s top labs: a large-scale LLM and reinforcement learning platform capable of training massive Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) models at frontier scale. We saw the effectiveness of our approach first-hand when we applied it to the critical domain of autonomous coding. With this milestone unlocked, we're now bringing these methods to general agentic reasoning.
  We've raised significant capital and identified a scalable commercial model that aligns with our open intelligence strategy, ensuring we can continue building and releasing frontier models sustainably. We are now scaling up to build open models that bring together large-scale pretraining and advanced reinforcement learning from the ground up.
  There is a window of opportunity today to build frontier open intelligence, but it is closing and this may be the last. If this mission resonates, join us. [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/reflectionai_today-were-sharing-the-next-phase-of-reflection-activity-7382075993852870656-9Bcn) [...more](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/reflectionai_today-were-sharing-the-next-phase-of-reflection-activity-7382075993852870656-9Bcn)

  From drone buddies to a homework reminder watch, I'm so impressed with all of the projects that came out of our recent Startup Kids Club event at [Lorong AI](https://sg.linkedin.com/company/lorong-ai?trk=public_profile__reactions-text) . Many kids used Replit for the first time to make games, educational apps, and tools to help them bond with their busy parents. Thank you to [Valerie Wagoner](https://sg.linkedin.com/in/valwagoner?trk=public_profile__reactions-text) and the Stanford Alumni community for collaborating on this event as well as the dozens of tech volunteers who made it all possible.
  During the event, the children selected a persona (a kid who's new to school, a kid who always forgets his homework, and other relatable characters) at the first of six stations. From there, they moved through the Ideas Station, Prototype Station, Clarify Station, Build Station, and Feedback Station. It was amazing to see how the kids related to the personas they'd chosen and to see how their idea evolved as they moved through the stations.
  Here’s a blog post I wrote, where you can find a select group of projects and the playbook for the event: [https://lnkd.in/gpgeTaue](https://lnkd.in/gpgeTaue?trk=public_profile__reactions-text)

  ### The Safe Autonomous Systems Lab
  
  1mo
  
  [Pieter Abbeel liked this](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/syherbert_congratulations-to-nikhil-shinde-when-we-activity-7437198574372667393-hSk9)
  
  Congratulations to [Nikhil Shinde](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nikhil-uday-shinde?trk=public_profile__reactions-text) , who defended his PhD thesis this week! His thesis is titled “Towards Safe Intelligent Robots in Uncertain and Unknown Environments”.
  I met Nikhil in my special topics course on safety for autonomous systems. He was in Professor [Michael Yip](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mcyip?trk=public_profile__reactions-text) ’s group and was interested in safe control of medical robots—an extremely hard topic for many reasons. He requested to be co-advised, and I jumped at the chance to work with him and Mike. Because of the difficult application Nikhil chose, the first few years were full of trying various approaches, hitting dead ends, and long brainstorming meetings to find new directions. Thanks to Nikhil’s tenacity and his strong ability to attract and collaborate with a diverse group of researchers, this exploration paid off and his productivity exploded, resulting in multiple papers and awards. In one year he went to something like 6 different countries to present his work!
  Congratulations to Dr. Nikhil Shinde!

* [Michael Johnston Detecon • 4K followers](https://nz.linkedin.com/in/mchaeljohnston?trk=public_profile_relatedPosts_face-pile-cta) [https://lnkd.in/efBuPQMn Is this the Horse to Back I recently came across a well-reasoned piece by Martin Milani critiquing Yann LeCun JEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture). Milani argues that while JEPA is a master of efficient perception, it lacks the explicit state, causal operators, and counterfactual machinery required to be a true "World Model." In his view, JEPA advances efficiency, not cognition. As a pragmatist looking at the future of telecommunications, I think Milani is right about the classification—but perhaps misses the massive opportunity that this "limitation" creates. We’ve spent years watching LLMs predict the next word in a sequence with remarkable fluency, yet they remain prone to hallucinations because they lack a grasp of physical reality. LeCun’s alternative, JEPA, ignores "pixel-perfect" detail to learn abstract representations of how the world works. If we view the future "Intelligence Stack" as a split system, the missing building block isn't a single, all-knowing model—it’s the network itself. (1) The Great Translator: - In my work consulting for telcos on the impact of AI, I’m increasingly convinced that the 6G network will become the "nervous system" where these models finally find their purpose. If a Mission-Critical World Model (System 2) is the Executive, then JEPA is the Sensory Cortex. The "symbol grounding problem" has long plagued logically perfect systems; they struggle to digest the messy, high-entropy noise of the physical world in real-time. This is where I believe that 6G and JEPA become codependent: JEPA as the Universal Semantic Interface: Its role isn't to provide fiduciary logic, but to act as a high-speed filter. It strips away surface-level noise and converts physical reality into "logic-ready" Latent Embeddings. Efficiency as an Enabler: - By handling the 99% of "intuitive physics" (the reflexes), JEPA allows the formal, heavy causal reasoning systems to focus only on the most relevant variables. (2) The Bridge to 6G: - The future isn't about JEPA evolving into a complete, autonomous reasoning agent, rather JEPA becoming the interface that allows logically grounded systems to "feel" and "react" to the physical world at scale. In a 6G-native environment, the network doesn't just move data; it processes perception / reacts to logic. JEPA provides the perceptual intuition, while the network provides the connectivity for the "fiduciary logic" of governance and trust to intervene when it matters most. I would like to think that we don't need JEPA to be the brain; we need it to be the bridge. 12 2 Comments](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mchaeljohnston_yann-lecun-just-raised-1bn-to-prove-the-activity-7437300975398178817-YSGu?trk=public_profile_relatedPosts)
* [John Byrd Gigantic Software, LLC • 2K followers](https://www.linkedin.com/in/john-byrd?trk=public_profile_relatedPosts_face-pile-cta) [Zero plus zero equals two. Not in some toy project. In Berkeley SoftFloat -- the IEEE 754 math library reference implementation. It's inside QEMU and most x86 emulators, and it's the oracle that hardware teams verify chip designs against. If you've ever emulated a CPU or validated a chip design in the last decade, Berkeley SoftFloat did the floating-point math. I found seven wrong results across five of six arithmetic operations in the 80-bit extended precision format. The one Intel invented for the x87 FPU in 1980. Some other fun highlights: \- 0.5 + 0.5 = 3 \- A huge finite number plus zero = infinity \- Infinity x 0 = infinity (and no error raised) \- Two tiny numbers added together = zero These aren't rounding errors. These are completely wrong answers for valid inputs. The root cause: the x87's 80-bit format has an explicit "integer bit" that every other IEEE format hides. This creates encodings -- unnormals, pseudo-denormals, pseudo-infinities -- where the bit says one thing and the exponent says another. The original 8087 handled all of them correctly. SoftFloat hasn't since its 2011 rewrite. Nobody noticed because the test suite has a structural blind spot. The test generator only produces the encodings that SoftFloat itself would output... the "nice" ones where the integer bit is consistent with the exponent. It never generates the inputs that trigger the bugs. I patched the generator to cover the full input space and failures lit up everywhere. I never would have found any of this if I hadn't been writing my own floating-point library from scratch. When my results disagreed with SoftFloat, I assumed I was wrong. Over and over. I'd go back to my code, recheck my math, trace through my logic... because the reference implementation couldn't possibly be wrong. That's what "reference" means. But the reference was wrong, and I wasn't, and suddenly... Suddenly I was very sad. SoftFloat is supposed to be "the thing that is correct." It's the ultimate tech industry oracle, the final reference on one plus one. TestFloat tests hardware against SoftFloat. FPGA developers validate against SoftFloat. When your personal deity lies, when addition and subtraction themselves dissemble, the epistemological foundation shifts under you. You can't trust the thing you trusted, and now you have to ask what else you can't trust. What makes my situation lonelier is that finding the bug doesn't feel like a win, because it shouldn't have been there in the first place. I wasn't looking for SoftFloat bugs.
